paper.title: An empirical consistent redshift bias: A possible direct reproducible observation of Zwicky's TL theory

person.name: Fritz Zwicky
sources: proposed tired-light theory in 1929, referenced in paper shamir-2024-redshift

theory.name: Tired Light Theory
theory.summary:
Theory proposed by Fritz Zwicky that photons lose energy as they travel through space,
causing redshift without requiring expansion. In extreme form, argues the Big Bang is
an artifact and the Universe is in steady state. The theory has lacked direct empirical
evidence until potentially this observation.

theory.name: Standard Cosmological Model (Lambda-CDM)
theory.summary:
The standard model of cosmology based on dark energy (Lambda) and cold dark matter (CDM).
Assumes redshift is primarily due to cosmic expansion and Hubble flow. Faces tensions
including H0 tension and unexpected mature galaxies at high redshifts observed by JWST.

assumption.statement: The rotational velocity of the Milky Way galaxy relative to observed galaxies has a negligible effect on redshift measurements
assumption.rationale: Standard redshift models assume rotational velocity is far smaller than linear velocity and can be ignored when measuring cosmological redshift

assumption.statement: Redshift corresponds primarily to the linear velocity of objects relative to Earth due to cosmic expansion
assumption.rationale: Standard cosmological model interprets redshift as Doppler effect from Hubble flow and cosmic expansion

claim.statement: Galaxies that rotate in the same direction relative to the Milky Way have higher mean redshift than galaxies rotating in the opposite direction, with the difference increasing at higher redshifts

The redshift bias is consistent across multiple datasets: SDSS, DESI Legacy Survey, HSC DR3, and third-party catalogs, using different telescopes, annotation methods, and covering both Northern and Southern Galactic poles
The redshift difference (Δz) between galaxies rotating in opposite directions increases with redshift, from ~0.0007-0.0016 at z<0.1 to ~0.0059-0.0070 at z=0.2-0.3

Redshift bias can explain the H0 tension: selecting SH0ES supernovae by rotation direction changes H0 from ~73.7 to ~69.05 km/s/Mpc (same direction) or ~74.2 km/s/Mpc (opposite direction), bringing same-direction value within error of CMB measurements
Galaxies rotating in opposite directions show ~10% flux differences in different filters, with larger absolute differences at shorter wavelengths

prediction.statement: The redshift bias will be more significant at higher redshifts (z>0.3) observed by JWST, potentially explaining mature galaxies in the early Universe

The observed 140% excess of galaxies rotating opposite to Milky Way in JWST deep fields is due to brightness differences from Doppler shift combined with tired-light effects that become more significant at greater distances

anomaly.name: H0 Tension
anomaly.description: Discrepancy between two different methods of measuring the Hubble constant: local distance ladder measurements (~73-74 km/s/Mpc) versus CMB measurements (~67 km/s/Mpc), suggesting different expansion rates and ages of the Universe

anomaly.name: Mature Galaxies at High Redshift
anomaly.description: JWST observations of mature, massive galaxies at redshifts z>11-15 that appear older than traditional galaxy formation models predict, challenging the standard cosmological timeline

anomaly.name: Redshift Bias from Rotational Velocity
anomaly.description: Systematic redshift difference between galaxies rotating in the same versus opposite direction relative to the Milky Way, with bias increasing at higher redshifts. This suggests redshift is not purely velocity-based and may be affected by rotational velocity or tired-light effects.

violated_assumption.violation_description: The observed redshift bias shows that rotational velocity of the Milky Way relative to observed galaxies does have a measurable effect on redshift, contradicting the assumption that it is negligible

The redshift bias that increases with distance (redshift) suggests redshift is not purely velocity-based, as the rotational velocity of the Milky Way is constant and cannot explain distance-dependent effects
